title: The case for municipal composting over individual recycling
summary: Argue whether cities should invest in centralised municipal composting systems rather than relying on individual household recycling as the primary strategy for reducing organic waste.
topic: The case for municipal composting over individual recycling
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) arguing that investment in centralised municipal composting systems is a more effective waste reduction strategy than relying on individual household recycling. Address the limitations of individual recycling — contamination rates, consumer confusion, export dependence — and consider the objections: cost, infrastructure requirements, and behaviour change. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete example. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What most recycling campaigns ask of individuals is a level of material knowledge that…
Admittedly, the capital cost of building centralised composting infrastructure is substantial, and…
Collected separately from dry waste, organic material can be processed into…

hintVocab.meaningVi: ủ phân hữu cơ theo quy mô đô thị / thành phố
rác thải hữu cơ (thức ăn thừa, chất thải thực vật)
tỷ lệ ô nhiễm / lẫn tạp chất
luồng tái chế (các vật liệu được thu gom để tái chế)
chuyển hướng rác thải khỏi bãi chôn lấp
phân hủy kỵ khí (phân hủy chất hữu cơ không có oxy)
xử lý ở quy mô lớn
sự thay đổi hành vi của hộ gia đình
thu gom rác tại lề đường / tại nhà
nhựa sinh học có thể ủ phân hữu cơ
khí thải methane
chuyển hướng rác thải hữu cơ
chất cải tạo đất (phân bón hoặc compost)
tái chế theo ý muốn (bỏ vào thùng rác bất cứ thứ gì mà không biết có tái chế được không)
chi phí vốn / chi tiêu vốn
khép vòng (hoàn thành chu trình tái sử dụng)
đầu tư cơ sở hạ tầng
sự phụ thuộc vào xuất khẩu (xuất khẩu rác tái chế ra nước ngoài)
cơ sở xử lý
kinh tế tuần hoàn

sampleText:
Household recycling has been the dominant model of consumer waste management in wealthy countries for three decades. It has also, by most measures, underperformed. Contamination rates in mixed recycling bins routinely run above twenty per cent; when China's 2018 National Sword policy restricted imports of contaminated recyclables, it exposed the degree to which recycling systems in Europe and North America had been exporting their problems rather than solving them. What the recycling model has largely asked individuals to do is master a set of material classifications — which plastics, which glass, which cardboard — that even trained waste professionals find complicated. The result is widespread wishful recycling: materials placed in the bin in the hope that someone downstream will sort it out.

Municipal composting operates on a different logic. Instead of asking residents to become expert sorters of dozens of material categories, it asks one thing: keep organic waste separate from dry waste. Collected separately at the kerbside and processed at scale in dedicated facilities, organic material can be converted into compost for agricultural use or, through anaerobic digestion, into biogas for energy. Cities that have invested in this model — including Seoul and San Francisco — have reported landfill diversion rates well above those achieved by equivalent recycling programmes. Seoul's food waste separation scheme, mandatory since 2013, now diverts over ninety-five per cent of the city's food waste from landfill, producing compost that is sold back to farms.

Admittedly, the capital expenditure required to build composting infrastructure is substantial. Processing facilities must be built, kerbside collection vehicles adapted, and — perhaps most importantly — residents persuaded to change behaviour. A city that has relied on a single mixed-waste bin for a generation faces real friction in moving to a separated system.

Even so, the infrastructure cost argument applies equally to recycling: sorting facilities, collection logistics, and consumer education campaigns also carry significant price tags, often without commensurate results. Having failed for three decades to make recycling work reliably at scale, cities can no longer treat infrastructure cost as an objection unique to composting.

Granted, composting does not address all waste streams; plastics, glass, and metals still require separate treatment. The case is not that composting replaces recycling wholesale but that it should replace recycling's position at the centre of waste strategy. Organic material makes up roughly forty per cent of household waste in most cities. Diverting it efficiently — through systems that do not depend on individual expertise — closes the loop where consumer recycling has most conspicuously failed to.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Contamination rates in mixed recycling bins routinely run above twenty per cent

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to expose a hidden demand of the recycling model
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the recycling model has largely asked individuals to do is master a set of material classifications' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is [infinitive]' để phơi bày yêu cầu ẩn của mô hình tái chế. Thay vì viết 'The recycling model asks individuals to master a complicated set of classifications', câu chẻ đôi đặt 'master a set of material classifications' ở cuối câu, làm cho sự phi thực tế của yêu cầu này trở nên rõ ràng hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the recycling model has largely asked individuals to do is master a set of material classifications' uses the what-cleft to expose the hidden demand of the recycling model. Instead of 'The recycling model asks individuals to master complicated classifications', the cleft places 'master a set of material classifications' at the end — making the unreasonableness of the demand more apparent.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Fronted perfect participle clause to dismiss a counter-argument
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having failed for three decades to make recycling work reliably at scale, cities can no longer treat infrastructure cost as an objection unique to composting' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành ('Having failed') để mở câu. Điều này thiết lập thực tế lịch sử trước — tái chế đã thất bại — trước khi rút ra hệ quả: chi phí cơ sở hạ tầng không thể là lý do từ chối ủ phân nếu chính tái chế cũng tốn kém tương tự.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having failed for three decades to make recycling work reliably at scale, cities can no longer treat infrastructure cost as an objection unique to composting' opens with a perfect participle clause. This establishes the historical record — recycling has failed — before drawing the logical consequence: infrastructure cost cannot be an objection to composting if recycling carries equivalent costs with worse results.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tái chế hộ gia đình đã là mô hình chủ đạo trong quản lý rác thải người tiêu dùng ở các nước giàu trong ba thập kỷ qua. Nó cũng, theo hầu hết các thước đo, đã hoạt động kém hơn kỳ vọng. Tỷ lệ ô nhiễm trong thùng tái chế hỗn hợp thường xuyên vượt quá hai mươi phần trăm; khi chính sách Kiếm Quốc gia năm 2018 của Trung Quốc hạn chế nhập khẩu vật liệu tái chế bị ô nhiễm, nó đã phơi bày mức độ mà các hệ thống tái chế ở châu Âu và Bắc Mỹ đã xuất khẩu vấn đề của họ thay vì giải quyết chúng. Điều mà mô hình tái chế phần lớn yêu cầu các cá nhân làm là thành thạo một bộ phân loại vật liệu — loại nhựa nào, loại thủy tinh nào, loại bìa nào — mà ngay cả các chuyên gia rác thải được đào tạo cũng thấy phức tạp. Kết quả là tái chế theo ý muốn phổ biến rộng rãi: vật liệu được đặt vào thùng rác với hy vọng rằng ai đó ở phía sau sẽ phân loại nó.

Ủ phân đô thị vận hành theo một logic khác. Thay vì yêu cầu cư dân trở thành những người phân loại chuyên nghiệp của hàng chục danh mục vật liệu, nó yêu cầu một điều: giữ rác thải hữu cơ tách biệt với rác thải khô. Được thu gom riêng tại lề đường và xử lý ở quy mô lớn trong các cơ sở chuyên dụng, chất hữu cơ có thể được chuyển đổi thành phân bón cho mục đích nông nghiệp hoặc, thông qua phân hủy kỵ khí, thành khí sinh học cho năng lượng. Các thành phố đã đầu tư vào mô hình này — bao gồm Seoul và San Francisco — đã báo cáo tỷ lệ chuyển hướng bãi chôn lấp cao hơn nhiều so với các chương trình tái chế tương đương. Chương trình phân loại rác thực phẩm của Seoul, bắt buộc từ năm 2013, hiện chuyển hướng hơn chín mươi lăm phần trăm rác thực phẩm của thành phố khỏi bãi chôn lấp, tạo ra phân bón được bán lại cho các trang trại.

Thừa nhận rằng, chi phí vốn cần thiết để xây dựng cơ sở hạ tầng ủ phân là đáng kể. Các cơ sở xử lý phải được xây dựng, các phương tiện thu gom tại lề đường phải được điều chỉnh, và — có lẽ quan trọng nhất — cư dân phải được thuyết phục thay đổi hành vi. Một thành phố đã dựa vào một thùng rác hỗn hợp duy nhất trong một thế hệ đối mặt với sự ma sát thực sự khi chuyển sang hệ thống tách biệt.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận về chi phí cơ sở hạ tầng áp dụng như nhau cho tái chế: các cơ sở phân loại, hậu cần thu gom và các chiến dịch giáo dục người tiêu dùng cũng mang lại chi phí đáng kể, thường mà không có kết quả tương xứng. Đã thất bại trong ba thập kỷ để làm cho tái chế hoạt động đáng tin cậy ở quy mô lớn, các thành phố không còn có thể coi chi phí cơ sở hạ tầng là sự phản đối chỉ riêng cho ủ phân.

Thừa nhận rằng, ủ phân không giải quyết tất cả các luồng rác thải; nhựa, thủy tinh và kim loại vẫn cần xử lý riêng. Lập luận không phải là ủ phân thay thế hoàn toàn tái chế mà là nó nên thay thế vị trí trung tâm của tái chế trong chiến lược rác thải. Chất hữu cơ chiếm khoảng bốn mươi phần trăm rác thải hộ gia đình ở hầu hết các thành phố. Chuyển hướng nó một cách hiệu quả — thông qua các hệ thống không phụ thuộc vào chuyên môn cá nhân — khép vòng nơi tái chế người tiêu dùng đã thất bại rõ ràng nhất.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to expose a hidden demand of the recycling model?
options: The recycling model asks individuals to master material classifications, which is what it has largely done.
What the recycling model has largely asked individuals to do is master a set of material classifications.
Mastering material classifications is largely what individuals are asked to do by the recycling model.
Material classifications are what the recycling model has largely asked individuals to master doing.
mcQuestions.explanation: The what-cleft 'What [subject + verb phrase] is [infinitive]' places 'master a set of material classifications' in the most emphatic final position, exposing the unreasonable demand.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the key claim as the final element.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a fronted perfect participle clause to build an argument from a track record?
options: Cities that have failed to make recycling work for three decades should now invest in composting.
Having failed for three decades to make recycling work reliably at scale, cities can no longer treat infrastructure cost as an objection unique to composting.
Having composting infrastructure as an alternative, cities have failed recycling for three decades.
Cities, failing for three decades in recycling, can no longer object to composting infrastructure costs.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having failed for three decades' is a fronted perfect participle clause that establishes the prior historical record before drawing the logical conclusion — infrastructure cost cannot be an objection unique to composting.
mcQuestions.hint: The fronted clause must begin with 'Having + past participle' and come before the main subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what happened after China's 2018 policy?
options: China's 2018 National Sword policy exposed how recycling systems in Europe and North America had been exporting their problems.
It exposed the degree to which recycling systems had been exporting their problems rather than solving them.
The problems were exported by recycling systems after China exposed the degree to which this was happening.
Recycling systems' problems, exposed by China's policy, had been exported rather than solved.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Recycling systems had been exporting their problems' uses passive-adjacent structure with a progressive perfect to foreground the ongoing, concealed nature of the practice — it had been happening for years without being noticed.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence keeps the focus on what recycling systems had been doing, without making China the main subject?

options: A campaign that encourages people to recycle by focusing on positive outcomes
Putting items in a recycling bin without knowing whether they can actually be recycled, in the hope they will be processed
A government scheme that rewards households for achieving high recycling rates
Recycling that is done selectively, only when it is convenient
mcQuestions.explanation: Wishful recycling (also called aspirational recycling) means placing items in recycling bins based on optimism rather than knowledge — the items may not be recyclable, but the person hopes someone will sort it out.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Wishful' suggests hoping rather than knowing — what kind of recycling is based on hope rather than certainty?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive participle clause as a fronted modifier?
options: Organic material, which is collected separately at the kerbside, can be converted into compost.
Collected separately at the kerbside and processed at scale in dedicated facilities, organic material can be converted into compost.
Organic material can be converted into compost when it is collected separately and processed at scale.
Separately collected and scaled-up processing, organic material converts into compost in dedicated facilities.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Collected separately at the kerbside and processed at scale in dedicated facilities' is a fronted passive participle phrase modifying 'organic material' — it establishes the conditions before the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: The fronted participle phrase comes before the main subject and describes what is done to it.

options: Rerouting lorries carrying waste to a different landfill site
Redirecting waste away from landfill sites through recycling, composting, or other treatment
The process of covering landfill sites with vegetation to reduce visual impact
Illegal dumping of waste in areas outside designated landfill zones
mcQuestions.explanation: Landfill diversion measures how much waste is kept out of landfill through alternative methods — a high diversion rate means little waste is being buried.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Diversion' means redirecting away from — redirecting waste away from what?

options: Admittedly, the capital expenditure required to build composting infrastructure is substantial.
The capital expenditure required admittedly to build composting infrastructure is substantial.
Capital expenditure, admittedly, required to build composting infrastructure substantial is.
Admittedly capital expenditure composting infrastructure is substantial to build required.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' is followed by a complete clause in standard word order — the standard concession structure.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Admittedly,', look for a grammatically complete subject-verb clause.

options: A process by which compost is spread on agricultural land to improve soil
The breakdown of organic material by microorganisms in the absence of oxygen, producing biogas and digestate
A medical treatment for digestive disorders caused by exposure to organic waste
The process by which plastic is broken down into smaller particles in the environment
mcQuestions.explanation: Anaerobic digestion is a biological process that breaks down organic matter without oxygen, producing biogas (used for energy) and a nutrient-rich digestate (used as fertiliser).
mcQuestions.hint: 'An-aerobic' means without air/oxygen — what happens to organic matter when it breaks down without oxygen?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'contamination rate' mean in the context of recycling?
options: The percentage of workers in recycling facilities who are exposed to hazardous materials
The proportion of material in a recycling stream that cannot be recycled because it is wrong, dirty, or mixed with non-recyclables
The rate at which recycled materials degrade over time
The percentage of recycling bins that are emptied on schedule each week
mcQuestions.explanation: A contamination rate measures how much of what people put in recycling bins cannot actually be recycled — because the wrong materials are included, materials are dirty, or items are not separated correctly.
mcQuestions.hint: Contamination means unwanted material that spoils the whole batch — what happens to a recycling batch when the wrong things are put in?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from a concession back to the main argument?
options: For instance, Seoul's composting programme has demonstrated very high diversion rates.
Even so, the infrastructure cost argument applies equally to recycling.
Admittedly, residents are often resistant to separating organic waste from dry waste.
In addition, composting also produces compost that can be sold back to farms.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the infrastructure cost concern and then turns it against the opposition — the same objection applies to recycling, which has produced worse results.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option says 'the objection is real, but it applies equally to the alternative you are defending'?

options: An economy in which all businesses operate in a single city centre district
An economic model in which materials are kept in use for as long as possible through repair, reuse, and recycling, minimising waste
A financial system in which money circulates only within a local community
An economy based on the production and export of renewable energy
mcQuestions.explanation: A circular economy aims to eliminate waste by keeping resources in use — through reducing consumption, reusing materials, and recycling — rather than the traditional linear 'take, make, dispose' model.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Circular' contrasts with 'linear' — instead of a straight line from resource to waste, what shape does the resource flow take?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, recycling is basically broken and everyone knows it — most of what you put in the bin just ends up in a landfill anyway.

The structural limitations of consumer-level recycling — including high contamination rates and dependence on international export markets — suggest a case for redirecting policy investment toward centralised organic waste treatment.
Recycling has kind of failed and composting is probably a better idea, especially because of what happened with China in 2018.
It's pretty clear that individual recycling doesn't really work that well and composting is a lot more effective.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Structural limitations', 'contamination rates', 'dependence on international export markets', and 'centralised organic waste treatment' are precise, formal collocations appropriate to academic argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', 'kind of', 'pretty clear', and 'doesn't really work that well'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'export dependency' mean in the context of recycling?
options: A country's dependence on exporting manufactured goods to other countries
The degree to which a country's recycling system relies on sending materials abroad for processing, rather than treating them domestically
The dependence of developing countries on importing recycling technology from wealthier nations
The practice of recycling companies exporting their profits to avoid domestic taxation
mcQuestions.explanation: Export dependency in recycling means that a country sends its collected recyclables to other countries for processing — a system that became vulnerable when China restricted these imports in 2018.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'export' + 'dependency' suggest about where the recycling actually happens?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses inversion after a restrictive negative adverb?
options: Rarely does a recycling programme achieve the diversion rates that a well-run composting scheme can deliver.
Rarely a recycling programme achieves the diversion rates that a well-run composting scheme can deliver.
A recycling programme rarely does achieve the diversion rates that a well-run composting scheme can deliver.
Rarely achieving diversion rates, a recycling programme does what a composting scheme can deliver.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Rarely', the auxiliary ('does') precedes the subject ('a recycling programme') — subject-auxiliary inversion after a negative or restrictive adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Rarely', does the auxiliary come before or after the subject?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to close the loop' mean in a waste management context?
options: To shut down a recycling facility when it reaches full capacity
To complete the cycle so that materials are returned to productive use rather than lost as waste
To seal a landfill site once it has been filled to capacity
To end a government recycling programme after it has achieved its targets
mcQuestions.explanation: Closing the loop means completing the circular cycle — organic matter becomes compost, which feeds soil, which grows food, which produces more organic matter — rather than sending material to landfill as a dead end.
mcQuestions.hint: A loop is a circle with no break — 'closing the loop' means what happens at the end connects back to the beginning.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best paraphrases 'systems that do not depend on individual expertise'?
options: Systems that are designed by waste management experts rather than government officials
Systems in which the effective outcome does not require each participant to possess specialist knowledge
Systems that automatically sort all waste types without any human involvement
Systems that have been tested by expert panels before being introduced to the public
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase argues that composting, unlike recycling, works without requiring each household to be an expert in material classification — the system is designed to be simple enough for ordinary people to use correctly.
mcQuestions.hint: The key contrast is between systems that need expert users and systems that work even without expert users — which is more scalable?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do you currently separate your household waste — or did you grow up in a household that did? On reflection, do you think the responsibility for effective waste management should rest primarily with individuals making correct daily choices, or with systems designed so that the correct choice is also the easy choice?
